---
title: "The Monte Carlo — Coverage Fails, AR Rescues"
date: "2026-07-06"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

# The Monte Carlo — Coverage Fails, AR Rescues {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Lab Three, part four of five — the heart of the lab. In part three we ran two-stage least squares by hand on one draw and saw it swing violently, with a tight standard error we were told to distrust. Now we average. We build the Anderson–Rubin interval, then run a Monte Carlo over more than a thousand draws. Coverage is the fraction of draws whose interval contained the true zero. We will watch the conventional interval fail that test badly, and Anderson–Rubin pass it honestly.
:::

## Anderson–Rubin, By Hand

- The disease: **dividing by a weak first stage**
- The cure: an inference that **never divides by the first stage**
- Test $H_0:\beta=\beta_0$ — form $y-\beta_0 x$, regress on $z$, $F$-test
- **Invert the test**: keep every $\beta_0$ not rejected at 5%

::: notes
The pathology came from one place: dividing by a weak, error-ridden first stage. So the cure is an inference procedure that never divides by the first stage. To test the hypothesis that beta equals some candidate value beta-naught, form the adjusted outcome y minus beta-naught times x, and regress it on z. If beta-naught is the true effect, then z — which only ever touched y through x — should have no relationship with the adjusted outcome, so its coefficient must be zero. You test that with an F-test. To get a confidence interval, you invert the test: sweep beta-naught over a grid and keep every value the test fails to reject at five percent. If the accepted set touches a grid edge, flag it unbounded — the honest signature of a weak instrument.
:::

## The Monte Carlo Engine

- At fixed `pi`, redraw the world `reps` times
- Each draw: record OLS, 2SLS, first-stage $F$
- Record whether conventional CI ($\hat\beta\pm 1.96\,\text{SE}$) covered $0$
- Record whether the AR interval covered $0$; `REPS = 1200`

::: notes
Now wrap one experiment. At a fixed pi, redraw the world twelve hundred times. On every draw, record the ordinary-least-squares estimate, the two-stage least-squares estimate, and the first-stage F. Then record two coverage indicators: whether the conventional ninety-five percent interval, beta-hat plus or minus one-point-nine-six standard errors, contained the true zero; and whether the Anderson–Rubin interval contained it. Coverage is the fraction of draws that covered. We run this at the weak setting. The output is, quite literally, Chapter three-point-five rendered in numbers.
:::

## What the Weak Monte Carlo Shows

- median OLS $= +0.400$ · median 2SLS $= +0.258$ (**between** OLS and 0)
- mean 2SLS $= -0.561$ — fat-tailed, **unstable**
- conventional 95% coverage $= 0.801$ — badly **under**
- AR coverage $= 0.973$ · AR unbounded on $0.911$ of draws

::: notes
Read every number, because this output is the lesson. The median ordinary-least-squares estimate sits at plus zero-point-four-zero — endogeneity, as before. The median two-stage least-squares estimate sits at plus zero-point-two-five-eight — strictly between the biased ordinary-least-squares value and the truth of zero. It did not purge the bias; it shrank it partway and stopped, retaining roughly sixty-five percent. That is the one-over-F-plus-one pull made visible. The conventional coverage is zero-point-eight-zero-one — a researcher reporting beta-hat plus or minus one-point-nine-six standard errors is wrong about their own uncertainty one time in five, not one in twenty. The Anderson–Rubin coverage is zero-point-nine-seven-three, back near the promised ninety-five, because it never divided by the weak first stage — at the price of being unbounded on ninety-one percent of draws, honesty about an effect this instrument cannot identify.
:::

## The Fat-Tail Subtlety

- mean 2SLS $= -0.561$ — wrong side of zero, far from the median
- 2SLS is a **ratio** with a near-zero denominator
- A few near-vanishing first stages throw huge values into the mean
- The **median** is the honest summary, not the mean

::: notes
Notice the mean two-stage least-squares estimate is minus zero-point-five-six-one — on the wrong side of zero and nowhere near the median of plus zero-point-two-six. That is the fat-tail subtlety. Two-stage least squares is a ratio with a near-zero denominator, so a few draws where the first stage nearly vanishes throw enormous values into the average and yank the mean around. The mean is not the honest summary; the median is. And the deeper moral: the conventional interval looks like a perfectly normal ninety-five percent interval on any single draw — tight, symmetric, often excluding zero — yet it covers only eighty percent of the time, because the true sampling distribution is fat-tailed, not the normal the formula assumes. The diagnostics that could warn you live outside the point estimate; the standard error itself never can.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Over 1,200 weak draws, median 2SLS ($+0.26$) sits between OLS ($+0.40$) and the truth ($0$), conventional coverage is only $\approx 0.80$, and AR coverage is $\approx 0.97$. The mean ($-0.56$) is meaningless — a fat-tailed ratio — so report the **median**.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part four: over twelve hundred weak draws, the median two-stage least-squares estimate sits strictly between the biased ordinary-least-squares value and the truth, retaining about sixty-five percent of the bias. The conventional interval covers only about eighty percent of the time, while Anderson–Rubin recovers about ninety-seven percent honest coverage. The mean is meaningless for this fat-tailed ratio, so we report the median. In part five we close the loop: the effective F that flags the weakness, then flip the knob to strong.
:::
